---
title: "Singapore Public Transport Ridership Recovery Across Bus, MRT & LRT"
subtitle: "AAI1001 Team Project Proposal"
author: "LAB-P1 Team 12"
date: "23 June 2026"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: false
    anchor-sections: true
    smooth-scroll: true
    page-layout: article
    theme: cosmo
    css: styles.css
    self-contained: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    df-print: paged
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

::: {.report-hero}
::: {.hero-kicker}
AAI1001 Team Project Proposal
:::

# Singapore Public Transport Ridership Recovery Across Bus, MRT & LRT

::: {.hero-author-label}
Author
:::

::: {.hero-author}
LAB-P1 Team 12
:::
:::

# Team Information

| Name | Student ID |
|---|---|
| Thaw Zin Htun | 2502712 |
| Siyona Shailesh Naik | 2502714 |
| Wilson Teng Hao Feng | 2503002 |
| Troy | XXXXXXX |
| Zayar | 2502688 |

# 1. Introduction and Project Overview

## 1.1 Our Project's Aim

Singapore's public transport ridership has recovered strongly since the disruption caused by Covid-19, but the recovery is not evenly distributed across buses, MRT and LRT. The selected Straits Times visualization is useful because it shows average daily rides from 2019 to 2025 in a single chart, allowing the 2019 pre-pandemic baseline to be compared with the later recovery years.

Our project aims to use this original visualization as the starting point for a clearer data story about public transport recovery in Singapore. The key issue is not only whether total ridership has returned to its pre-pandemic level, but also how the recovery differs by transport mode. In the chart, MRT ridership in 2025 is above its 2019 level, bus ridership remains below its 2019 level, and LRT remains much smaller in scale while slipping slightly from 2024 to 2025.

The improved visualization will therefore focus on four connected dimensions: **year**, **transport mode**, **average daily ridership**, and **recovery relative to 2019**. This allows the project to move beyond a simple total-ridership comparison and examine how Singapore's ridership share by transport mode has changed over time.

::: {.question-grid}
::: {.question-card}
<span class="question-index">Q1</span>
How did Singapore's average daily public transport ridership change from 2019 to 2025?
:::

::: {.question-card}
<span class="question-index">Q2</span>
How did bus, MRT and LRT ridership recover differently relative to the 2019 baseline?
:::

::: {.question-card}
<span class="question-index">Q3</span>
Has the overall ridership share by transport mode changed even though total ridership has nearly recovered?
:::
:::

## 1.2 Source & Context

::: {.source-card}
<span class="source-label">Original article</span>
**["More rides on MRT in 2025 as LRT use fell; bus ride numbers still below pre-Covid-19 levels"](https://www.straitstimes.com/singapore/transport/more-people-used-mrt-in-2025-as-lrt-ridership-falls-bus-numbers-still-below-pre-covid-levels)**  
Published by *The Straits Times* on 30 January 2026. The article was written by Vanessa Paige Chelvan and reports Land Transport Authority (LTA) ridership figures.
:::

The chosen chart is a single quantitative news-media visualization about Singapore's public transport ridership. It shows the average number of daily rides, in thousands, for public bus, MRT and LRT services from 2019 to 2025. The underlying figures correspond to LTA's official **Average Public Transport Ridership By Year (2015 to 2025)** table, while the Straits Times article provides the news context and interpretation.

The key 2025 figures are consistent across the article, the chart and LTA's published data: MRT ridership reached **3.490 million daily trips**, bus ridership averaged **3.841 million daily trips**, and LRT ridership was **209,000 daily trips**. Compared with the 2019 baseline, MRT ridership increased from **3.384 million** to **3.490 million**, bus ridership remained below its 2019 level of **4.099 million**, and LRT was almost unchanged from **208,000** in 2019 after dipping slightly from **210,000** in 2024.

The topic is suitable for this project because it is quantitative, current and socially relevant. Public transport ridership reflects changes in commuting behaviour, remote work, rail network expansion, first- and last-mile travel, and post-pandemic mobility patterns. The original chart already communicates the broad recovery trend, but it creates opportunities for improvement because recovery relative to 2019 and mode-level changes are not equally easy to compare.

::: {.data-source-grid}
::: {.data-source-card}
<span class="card-kicker">Primary data direction</span>
**LTA yearly public transport ridership**  
Average daily rides by year and transport mode, measured in thousands.
:::

::: {.data-source-card}
<span class="card-kicker">Supporting data direction</span>
**LTA monthly public transport ridership**  
Monthly average daily rides by transport mode, used only for optional context and checking.
:::

::: {.data-source-card}
<span class="card-kicker">Analytical focus</span>
**Recovery and ridership share**  
Mode share, year-on-year change and recovery index against 2019.
:::
:::

Together, these sources allow the project to move beyond a simple stacked total and examine recovery by mode, mode share and year-on-year change.

## 1.3 Our Chosen Visualization

![Original Straits Times visualization, "Public transport: Average number of rides per day (in '000)". Source: Straits Times Graphics; data source: Land Transport Authority.](ChosenVisualization.png){#fig-chosen-visualization fig-align="center" width="82%"}

### 1.3.1 Visualization Structure and Design

::: {.figure-shell}
::: {.figure-header}
<span class="figure-title">Original chart structure</span>
<span class="figure-subtitle">Horizontal stacked bars, one row per year, showing bus, MRT and LRT ridership.</span>
:::

| Encoding | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Vertical row position | Year from 2019 to 2025 |
| Horizontal stacked bar length | Total average daily ridership for that year |
| Segment length | Average daily ridership for each transport mode, measured in thousands |
| Colour hue | Transport mode: Bus, MRT or LRT |
| Direct text labels | Total ridership beside each year; bus and MRT values inside the bar segments |
| Colour intensity | Latest year, 2025, is visually emphasized; earlier years are faded |

::: {.figure-note}
The original chart is useful as a compact overview, but some analytical comparisons, especially recovery relative to 2019 and the small LRT values, are not directly shown.
:::
:::

The original visualization is structured as a set of horizontal stacked bars, one row per year:

- Each row represents one year from 2019 to 2025.
- The bold number beside each year shows the total average daily ridership across bus, MRT and LRT, in thousands.
- Each stacked bar is divided into three coloured segments: dark blue for bus, blue for MRT and grey for LRT.
- The bus and MRT segments include direct value labels, while the LRT segment is only shown as a small grey segment at the end of each bar.
- The 2025 row uses stronger colour saturation than earlier years, directing attention to the most recent ridership figures.

The chart directly encodes **three dimensions** and **one aggregate measure**. It also supports a derived comparison against the 2019 baseline, but that recovery comparison is not explicitly encoded in the original design:

::: {.comparison-table}
| Dimension (Bolded) / Comparison | Type | How It Appears in the Original Chart |
|---|---|---|
| **Year** | Temporal / ordinal | One horizontal row for each year from 2019 to 2025 |
| **Transport mode** | Categorical | Colour-coded stacked segments for Bus, MRT and LRT |
| **Average daily ridership by mode** | Quantitative measure | Segment lengths, with direct labels for bus and MRT |
| Total average daily ridership | Aggregate quantitative measure | Bold total number beside each year and overall stacked bar length |
| Recovery relative to 2019 | Derived comparison, not directly encoded | Readers must compare later rows with the 2019 row manually |
:::

This structure makes the chart effective for showing the broad recovery pattern from 2019 to 2025. However, because the chart is a stacked bar chart, bus is the only mode with a consistent left baseline. MRT and LRT begin after earlier segments, making precise year-to-year mode comparison harder. The absence of direct LRT labels also means readers must subtract bus and MRT values from the total if they want exact LRT figures.

### 1.3.2 The Story It Tells

::: {.story-panel}
::: {.story-stat}
<span class="story-value">7.691M → 5.040M → 7.540M</span>
<span class="story-label">Total daily public transport rides fell sharply in 2020, then recovered to about 98.0% of the 2019 level by 2025.</span>
:::
:::

The original visualization shows a clear pattern about Singapore's public transport recovery after Covid-19. The overall recovery looks strong, but the mode-level details show that ridership did not return in exactly the same pattern as before the pandemic.

**Total ridership nearly recovered.** Total average daily public transport ridership fell from **7.691 million rides in 2019** to **5.040 million rides in 2020**, before rising to **7.540 million rides in 2025**. This means 2025 ridership was about **98.0% of the 2019 level**.

**MRT recovered more strongly than buses.** MRT ridership increased from **3.384 million daily rides in 2019** to **3.490 million in 2025**, exceeding its pre-pandemic level by about **106,000 daily rides**. Bus ridership remained below its 2019 level, decreasing from **4.099 million** to **3.841 million**, a gap of about **258,000 daily rides**.

**The balance between transport modes changed.** MRT's share of total ridership increased, while bus share decreased. Buses accounted for about **53.3%** of daily rides in 2019 but about **50.9%** in 2025, while MRT rose from about **44.0%** to **46.3%**. LRT stayed much smaller in scale, at **209,000 daily rides in 2025**, almost unchanged from **208,000 in 2019**.

Overall, the original chart gives a useful yearly overview of public transport recovery, but the current design makes the reader work too hard to extract the most important analytical comparisons. Our project will preserve the useful yearly overview while improving readability, highlighting recovery relative to 2019, and making changes in ridership share by transport mode easier to compare.

# 2. Critical Analysis of Original Visualization

::: {.section-lead}
This section reviews the original Straits Times chart by looking at both its useful features and its design limitations. The strengths show which parts of the chart should be retained, while the weaknesses highlight the areas that need improvement in the redesigned visualization.
:::

### 2.1 Strengths

What works well in the original chart

::: {.critique-grid}
::: {.critique-card .positive}
#### <span class="critique-id positive-id">S1.</span> Total ridership and mode split are shown together

The chart's strongest feature is that it shows both the total number of average daily rides and the split between bus, MRT and LRT in one compact view. This is useful because the project story depends on both the overall recovery and the changing contribution of each mode. It also helps readers see that similar total ridership levels can still have different ridership distributions across transport modes.
:::

::: {.critique-card .positive}
#### <span class="critique-id positive-id">S2.</span> The year-by-year layout makes the recovery trend easy to follow

The rows are arranged by year from 2025 at the top to 2019 at the bottom, allowing readers to scan the post-pandemic fall and recovery across time. The large drop in 2020 and the gradual return toward the 2019 level are visible without needing a separate time-series chart.
:::

::: {.critique-card .positive}
#### <span class="critique-id positive-id">S3.</span> Consistent colour mapping supports mode recognition

Bus, MRT and LRT use the same colours across all years. Even though the older rows are faded, the repeated colour mapping helps readers recognise the same transport modes throughout the chart.
:::

::: {.critique-card .positive}
#### <span class="critique-id positive-id">S4.</span> Key labels and source information are included

The title, subtitle, legend and source note give readers the basic context needed to interpret the chart. The subtitle states the unit, "Average number of rides per day (in '000)", and the source note credits the Land Transport Authority.
:::
:::

### 2.2 Weaknesses

What limits deeper interpretation

::: {.critique-grid}
::: {.critique-card .risk}
#### <span class="critique-id risk-id">W1.</span> The 2019 baseline is not visually marked

The article's story depends on whether ridership has returned to pre-Covid-19 levels, but the 2019 row is not marked as a baseline. Readers must manually compare 2020-2025 values against 2019 to judge recovery.
:::

::: {.critique-card .risk}
#### <span class="critique-id risk-id">W2.</span> Stacked bars make MRT and LRT comparisons less direct

In a stacked bar chart, only the first segment starts from a common baseline. Bus is easier to compare across years because it begins at the same left edge, while MRT and LRT begin after the preceding segments. This makes year-to-year comparison for MRT and LRT less precise.
:::

::: {.critique-card .risk}
#### <span class="critique-id risk-id">W3.</span> LRT is visually compressed and not directly labelled

LRT ridership is much smaller than bus and MRT ridership, so its grey segment appears as a thin end strip. The chart also does not label LRT values directly, making the 2024 to 2025 change from 210,000 to 209,000 rides almost impossible to detect visually.
:::

::: {.critique-card .risk}
#### <span class="critique-id risk-id">W4.</span> Recovery gaps and year-to-year changes are not calculated for the reader

The chart shows raw ridership values but does not show how far each mode is from its 2019 level or how values changed from year to year. Important comparisons, such as MRT being about 106,000 rides above 2019 while bus remains about 258,000 rides below 2019, have to be calculated separately by the reader.
:::

::: {.critique-card .risk}
#### <span class="critique-id risk-id">W5.</span> Changes in ridership share are hidden inside totals

The chart gives total ridership and mode values, but it does not directly show each mode's percentage share of total ridership. As a result, the shift from bus toward MRT is present in the data but not immediately visible.
:::

::: {.critique-card .risk}
#### <span class="critique-id risk-id">W6.</span> Faded older rows and low contrast reduce precise comparison

The stronger colour in the 2025 row successfully draws attention to the latest year, but the faded older rows make exact cross-year comparison harder. This also creates a readability and accessibility concern, especially for the small grey LRT segment, which is both small and low contrast.
:::
:::

::: {.recommendation-callout}
**Redesign direction.** The improved visualization should retain strengths S1 to S4 while addressing weaknesses W1 to W6 through a clearer 2019 baseline comparison, easier mode-level reading, direct LRT labelling, recovery-gap metrics and visible changes in ridership share by transport mode. This will help readers see what has recovered, what remains below the pre-pandemic level, and how the balance between transport modes has changed.
:::

# 3. Proposed Improvements

::: {.section-lead}
Based on the weaknesses identified in Section 2, our redesign will keep the final output as **one plot/chart**, not a dashboard or a collection of separate charts. The improved visualization will use one interactive chart to make the 2019 baseline, mode-level recovery gaps, LRT values and changes in ridership share by transport mode easier to compare.
:::

## 3.1 Redesign Concept

The proposed redesign is a single interactive recovery-index chart. The default view will show public transport ridership recovery indexed to **2019 = 100**, with one line for each transport mode: Bus, MRT and LRT. An optional indexed total ridership line may also be included or toggled on and off if it does not overcrowd the chart.

This design directly supports the project story because 2019 is the pre-Covid baseline. A value above 100 means ridership has exceeded its 2019 level, while a value below 100 means ridership is still below its 2019 level. This makes the recovery pattern easier to read than the original stacked bar chart, where readers must manually compare each later year against 2019.

Interactivity will support the main chart without turning it into a dashboard. Hover tooltips can show exact average daily rides, ridership share by transport mode, year-on-year change and recovery gap from 2019. If technically feasible, a simple metric control may allow the same chart to switch between **recovery index**, **absolute ridership** and **ridership share by transport mode**, while still remaining one chart object.

## 3.2 Improvement Plan

| Weakness Addressed | Proposed Improvement | Method / Technique |
|---|---|---|
| <span class="critique-id risk-id">**W1, W4**</span> | Make the 2019 baseline explicit. The original chart includes 2019, but it does not clearly mark it as the reference year for judging recovery. The redesigned chart will make 2019 the starting baseline for all modes. | Use a recovery index where **2019 = 100**; add a horizontal reference line at 100; use endpoint labels to show whether 2025 is above or below the baseline. |
| <span class="critique-id risk-id">**W2**</span> | Replace stacked segment comparison with common-baseline mode comparison. In the original stacked bar chart, bus is easier to compare than MRT and LRT because only bus starts from the same left edge. The improved chart will give each mode the same recovery-index baseline. | Use a line-and-point chart with year on the x-axis and recovery index on the y-axis; show bus, MRT and LRT as separate coloured lines. |
| <span class="critique-id risk-id">**W3**</span> | Make LRT readable despite its small absolute size. In the original chart, LRT appears as a thin grey segment because its ridership is much smaller than bus and MRT. The redesigned chart will allow LRT to be read as its own trend instead of being visually compressed. | Show LRT as its own line on the indexed scale; add hover tooltip values and selected endpoint labels for exact LRT ridership. |
| <span class="critique-id risk-id">**W4**</span> | Add computed recovery metrics instead of requiring manual calculation. The original chart shows raw ridership values, but it does not calculate how far each mode is from its 2019 level. | Derive recovery index, absolute gap from 2019 and percentage change from 2019; show these values in tooltips and selected annotations. |
| <span class="critique-id risk-id">**W5**</span> | Show changes in ridership share by transport mode. The original chart contains the data needed to see that MRT's share increased while bus share decreased, but this pattern is hidden inside the stacked totals. | Calculate each mode's percentage share of total ridership; show share values in tooltips; optionally allow a same-chart metric toggle to display ridership share. |
| <span class="critique-id risk-id">**W6**</span> | Improve readability and accessibility while preserving mode identity. The original chart uses strong colour for 2025 and faded colours for older years, which draws attention to the latest year but makes comparison harder. | Use consistent colour mapping for transport modes, stronger contrast, clearer labels and a colourblind-conscious palette. |

## 3.3 How the Single Chart Will Work

The improved chart will use the following encodings:

| Chart Element | Meaning |
|---|---|
| X-axis | Year, from 2019 to 2025 |
| Y-axis | Recovery index, where 2019 = 100 |
| Line colour | Transport mode: bus, MRT or LRT |
| Points | Yearly observations |
| Horizontal reference line | 2019 baseline at 100 |
| Endpoint labels | 2025 recovery status for each mode |
| Hover tooltip | Exact ridership, recovery index, gap from 2019, year-on-year change and ridership share |

The default recovery-index view will make the main comparison immediate. MRT should appear above the 100 baseline by 2025, showing that it exceeded its 2019 ridership level. Bus should remain below 100, showing that it has not fully returned to its 2019 level. LRT will be easier to inspect because it will no longer be compressed into a small end segment of a stacked bar.

## 3.4 Original Strengths Preserved

::: {.critique-card .positive}
**<span class="critique-id positive-id">S1.</span> Total ridership and mode-level context are retained.** The redesigned chart will still allow readers to understand both overall ridership recovery and differences between bus, MRT and LRT. Total ridership can be included as an optional line or displayed in the tooltip.

**<span class="critique-id positive-id">S2.</span> Year-by-year recovery remains easy to follow.** The improved chart keeps the same 2019 to 2025 time frame, so readers can still follow the drop in 2020 and the gradual recovery in later years.

**<span class="critique-id positive-id">S3.</span> Consistent colour mapping is preserved.** Each transport mode will continue to use a consistent colour across the chart. This helps readers track bus, MRT and LRT throughout the full period.

**<span class="critique-id positive-id">S4.</span> Labels and source information remain clear.** The improved visualization will keep a clear title, subtitle, legend, unit explanation and source note. It will also add more useful labels and hover details, such as the 2019 baseline, 2025 endpoint values and recovery gaps.
:::

## 3.5 Expected Outcome

The redesigned chart will answer the project question more directly: has Singapore's public transport ridership recovered relative to 2019, and did the recovery differ across bus, MRT and LRT?

Compared with the original stacked bar chart, the improved visualization will reduce manual calculation, make mode-level comparison clearer, and make the small LRT trend easier to read. It will also show that total public transport ridership almost recovered by 2025, but the recovery pattern changed because MRT rose above its 2019 level while bus ridership remained below it.

# 4. Data Sources & Data Quality

## 4.1 Selected Datasets

::: {.section-lead}
The improved visualization will use official Land Transport Authority ridership data as the numerical source. The Straits Times article and original chart are retained as the news-media source that defines the project story, but the final plotted values will come from LTA rather than being visually estimated from the image.
:::

Primary Dataset

| Field | Details |
|---|---|
| Dataset name | Average Public Transport Ridership By Year (2015 to 2025) |
| Publisher | Land Transport Authority (LTA), Singapore |
| Source | [LTA Statistics & Publications](https://www.lta.gov.sg/content/ltagov/en/who_we_are/statistics_and_publications/statistics.html), Public Transport Ridership section. Direct file: [Average Daily Public Transport Ridership by Year PDF](https://www.lta.gov.sg/content/dam/ltagov/who_we_are/statistics_and_publications/statistics/pdf/PT_Ridership_Yearly_2015-2025.pdf) |
| CSV Source | [LTA DataMall Static Datasets](https://datamall.lta.gov.sg/content/datamall/en/static-data.html) as "Public Transport Utilisation - Average Public Transport Ridership by Year" |
| Format | CSV for data pipeline; PDF table for official reference and value checking |
| Unit | Average number of rides per day. The DataMall CSV stores full ride counts; `daily_rides_k` will be derived by dividing `ridership` by 1,000. |
| Coverage used | 2019 to 2025, matching the original Straits Times chart |
| Variables needed | `year`, `mode`, `ridership` |
| Role in project | Main dataset for the final chart. It will be used to calculate recovery index, 2019 gap, year-on-year change, total ridership and ridership share by transport mode. |

Supporting Dataset

| Field | Details |
|---|---|
| Dataset name | Average Public Transport Ridership By Month (2019 to 2025) |
| Publisher | Land Transport Authority (LTA), Singapore |
| Source | [LTA Statistics & Publications](https://www.lta.gov.sg/content/ltagov/en/who_we_are/statistics_and_publications/statistics.html), Public Transport Ridership section. Direct file: [Average Daily Public Transport Ridership by Month PDF](https://www.lta.gov.sg/content/dam/ltagov/who_we_are/statistics_and_publications/statistics/pdf/PT_Ridership_Monthly_2019-2025.pdf) |
| CSV Source | The same monthly ridership dataset is also available on [LTA DataMall Static Datasets](https://datamall.lta.gov.sg/content/datamall/en/static-data.html) as "Public Transport Utilisation - Average Public Transport Ridership by Month", but the CSV version is currently only updated to 2024. |
| Format | PDF table for 2019 to 2025; DataMall CSV for 2019 to 2024 |
| Unit | Average number of rides per day, in thousands |
| Coverage used | 2019 to 2025 |
| Variables needed | Month, year, Public Bus ridership, MRT ridership, LRT ridership |
| Role in project | Supporting source for validation and context. It can be used to check whether the annual recovery pattern is consistent with monthly ridership trends, but the final proposed chart will use the official yearly dataset as the authoritative source. |

Original News-Media Source

| Field | Details |
|---|---|
| Article | ["More rides on MRT in 2025 as LRT use fell; bus ride numbers still below pre-Covid-19 levels"](https://www.straitstimes.com/singapore/transport/more-people-used-mrt-in-2025-as-lrt-ridership-falls-bus-numbers-still-below-pre-covid-levels) |
| Publisher | The Straits Times |
| Author and date | Vanessa Paige Chelvan, 30 January 2026 |
| Role in project | Defines the original news-media visualization, topic framing and critique target. It is not treated as the main numerical dataset because the underlying official ridership values are available from LTA. |

## 4.2 Justification for Dataset Selection

The yearly LTA ridership dataset is the most appropriate primary source because it directly matches the scope of the original visualization: yearly average daily ridership for public bus, MRT and LRT services. Using the official LTA table avoids estimating values from the Straits Times image and gives the project a reproducible numerical foundation.

The dataset also supports the proposed redesign in Section 3. From the raw yearly values, we can create the measures needed for the interactive recovery-index chart. These measures will be fully derived in the later data engineering workflow, but they are listed here to show why the yearly dataset is suitable for the redesign.

| Measure Needed                    | Purpose in the Improved Visualization                         |
| --------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Total ridership                   | Preserves the original chart's total-ridership context        |
| Recovery index                    | Makes the 2019 baseline explicit and comparable across modes  |
| Gap from 2019                     | Shows how far each mode is above or below the pre-Covid level |
| Year-on-year change               | Supports tooltip explanation of recovery momentum             |
| Ridership share by transport mode | Shows whether the balance between Bus, MRT and LRT changed    |

The monthly LTA dataset is useful as a supporting source, but it should not replace the annual table for the main chart. Monthly values can help check the annual recovery pattern and provide context if needed. However, since the proposed final visualization is annual, the official yearly table will remain the authoritative source for the plotted values.

Together, the selected sources satisfy the project requirements: the original visualization comes from an online news-media source, while the curated data for the improved visualization comes from an official public data source that supports cleaning, transformation and analysis.

## 4.3 Raw Data Structure and Known Challenges

The raw data is compact but still requires careful handling. The primary yearly data from LTA DataMall is already available as a machine-readable CSV in long format, with one row per year and transport mode. The LTA Statistics PDF will still be kept as an official reference for checking values. We will therefore document source selection, unit handling, mode labels and validation checks carefully before creating the visualization-ready dataset.

### 4.3.1 Public Transport Ridership Raw Layout

The primary yearly CSV contains one row per year and transport mode. The useful raw structure is:

| Raw Column | Meaning | Planned Clean Column |
|---|---|---|
| year | Calendar year | `year` |
| mode | Transport mode, such as Public Bus, MRT or LRT | `mode` |
| ridership | Average daily ridership, stored as full ride counts | `ridership` |

For readability and value checking, the 2019 to 2025 subset is shown below in the same thousand-based format used in the original Straits Times chart. This table is not the raw CSV structure. It is a summary view after converting the CSV ridership values into daily_rides_k = ridership / 1000 and arranging the transport modes as columns.

| Year | Public Bus ('000) | MRT ('000) | LRT ('000) | Total ('000) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 2019 | 4,099 | 3,384 | 208 | 7,691 |
| 2020 | 2,878 | 2,023 | 139 | 5,040 |
| 2021 | 3,008 | 2,100 | 151 | 5,259 |
| 2022 | 3,461 | 2,745 | 184 | 6,390 |
| 2023 | 3,747 | 3,243 | 202 | 7,192 |
| 2024 | 3,837 | 3,412 | 210 | 7,459 |
| 2025 | 3,841 | 3,490 | 209 | 7,540 |

### 4.3.2 Supporting Context Data Raw Layout

The monthly LTA source has a similar structure but is organised by month rather than by year. It will be treated as supporting context only unless we decide to add monthly validation in the final analysis.

| Raw Column | Meaning | Planned Clean Column |
|---|---|---|
| Month / Year | Monthly observation date | `month`, `year`, `date` |
| Public Bus | Average daily public bus rides for the month, in thousands | `bus_rides_k` |
| MRT | Average daily MRT rides for the month, in thousands | `mrt_rides_k` |
| LRT | Average daily LRT rides for the month, in thousands | `lrt_rides_k` |

The Straits Times article and local `ChosenVisualization.png` image will be used only as documentation of the original visualization. We will not digitise values from the image for the final dataset because the official LTA source provides the underlying data directly.

### 4.3.3 Identified Data Quality Challenges

| Issue | Severity | Planned Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| The primary yearly data is available as a machine-readable CSV, while the LTA PDF is used as an official reference table. The two sources must be interpreted consistently because the CSV stores full ride counts while the PDF/chart display values in thousands. | <span class="severity-medium">Medium</span> | Use the yearly CSV as the main data pipeline source. Keep the yearly PDF as a reference copy and derive `daily_rides_k = ridership / 1000` for comparison with the original chart. |
| Units are published in thousands of average daily rides. It is easy to mix up thousands, millions and raw ride counts. | <span class="severity-high">High</span> | Store ridership values in a clearly named `*_rides_k` format, and create formatted labels such as "3.490M" only at the visualization stage. |
| The LTA source labels bus ridership as "Public Bus", while the Straits Times chart labels it as "Bus". | <span class="severity-low">Low</span> | Standardise the mode label to `Bus` in the final chart, while documenting that it comes from LTA's `Public Bus` mode value. |
| Annual values are average daily ridership, not total annual passenger trips. | <span class="severity-high">High</span> | Do not interpret the figures as yearly totals. Titles, subtitles, tooltips and captions will state "average daily rides" clearly. |
| Monthly values should not be averaged naively to recreate annual values because months have different numbers of days. | <span class="severity-medium">Medium</span> | Use the official annual dataset as the authoritative source for the final chart. If monthly data is used for validation, treat it as contextual rather than as a replacement for annual figures. |
| LRT values are small relative to bus and MRT, so rounding can make small differences look more precise than they are. | <span class="severity-medium">Medium</span> | Label LRT values clearly, but interpret small changes such as 210,000 to 209,000 rides cautiously. Use the recovery-index view to make the trend readable without overstating the magnitude. |
| The latest available yearly dataset may be updated by LTA in future releases. | <span class="severity-low">Low</span> | Record the retrieval date and source links in the references. If the LTA file changes, rerun the cleaning pipeline and compare outputs before updating the final visualization. |

## 4.4 Data Quality Summary

Overall, the selected LTA datasets are suitable because they are official, directly related to the original Straits Times chart and use consistent transport-mode categories. The main data quality issues are not about missing data, but about correct interpretation and careful preparation. The most important issues are the unit of measurement, the use of 2019 as the baseline, the small scale of LRT ridership and the need to calculate recovery comparisons clearly.

These issues will be handled in the later data engineering workflow so that the final visualization can show recovery relative to 2019, differences between Bus, MRT and LRT, and changes in ridership share by transport mode.

# 5. Data Engineering Workflow

## 5.1 Planned Data Engineering Pipeline

The data engineering workflow will convert the LTA DataMall yearly ridership CSV into a clean, visualization-ready dataset for the proposed one-chart redesign. The yearly CSV is already in long format, with one row per year and transport mode. Therefore, the workflow will focus on filtering the relevant years, standardising labels and units, validating the official values, and deriving the recovery metrics needed for the interactive chart.

The Straits Times article and `ChosenVisualization.png` will be used as documentation of the original visualization. They will not be used as the numerical source because the LTA yearly table provides the underlying values directly.

**Pipeline principle:** each stage will produce a named intermediate object so that the process is reproducible, inspectable and easy to rerun. Raw extracted values will remain separate from cleaned values, and derived analytical fields will be created only after the basic data structure has been validated.

| Stage | Input | Main Operations | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Ingest yearly CSV | LTA DataMall yearly ridership CSV | Read the CSV file containing `year`, `mode` and `ridership`; keep the raw file unchanged for traceability | `raw_yearly_ridership` |
| 2. Filter and standardise data | `raw_yearly_ridership` | Filter years 2019 to 2025; keep only Public Bus, MRT and LRT; rename `Public Bus` to `Bus`; convert `year` and `ridership` to numeric fields if needed | `clean_yearly_long` |
| 3. Standardise units and validate values | `clean_yearly_long` | Keep the raw `ridership` value as full average daily rides; create `daily_rides_k = ridership / 1000` to match the original chart's thousands unit; check row counts and known 2025 values | `clean_yearly_long` validated |
| 4. Derive analytical metrics | `clean_yearly_long` | Calculate yearly total ridership, ridership share by transport mode, 2019 baseline by mode, recovery index, gap from 2019, percentage change from 2019 and year-on-year change | `ridership_metrics` |
| 5. Prepare chart objects | `ridership_metrics` | Create tooltip fields, endpoint labels, optional total-ridership series and metric-toggle data if implemented | `ridership_data`, `mode_labels` |
| 6. Validate and export | `ridership_metrics`, `ridership_data`, `mode_labels` | Check missing values, row counts, 2019 baseline values, mode-share sums and known 2025 totals before exporting processed data | `validation_checks`, processed CSV/RDS files |

## 5.2 Planned Cleaning and Transformation Decisions

Every cleaning decision below is tied to a known data issue from Section 4 or a design issue from Section 2. The key principle is to avoid hidden unit conversion or manual calculation inside the chart code.

| Decision | Issue Handled | Planned Treatment | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | The main yearly data is available as a DataMall CSV, while the LTA PDF remains the official reference table | Use the CSV for the data pipeline and keep the PDF as a reference for checking values | This avoids manual transcription from the PDF while still allowing the team to verify that the CSV values match the official published table |
| D2 | The CSV stores ridership as full average daily ride counts, while the original chart displays values in thousands | Keep the raw `ridership` column unchanged and create `daily_rides_k = ridership / 1000` for chart comparison | This prevents confusion between full ride counts, thousands and millions |
| D3 | Source labels and chart labels differ slightly | Map LTA's `Public Bus` mode value to the final chart label `Bus`; keep MRT and LRT unchanged | The shorter label is clearer in the chart while the data dictionary preserves the source meaning |
| D4 | The DataMall yearly CSV is already in long format | Keep the long structure with `year`, `mode` and `daily_rides_k` instead of pivoting from wide format | The existing long format already supports one line per mode in the recovery-index chart |
| D5 | The recovery story depends on a pre-Covid baseline | Use each mode's own 2019 value as its baseline | This ensures Bus, MRT and LRT are compared against their own pre-Covid ridership levels rather than against the total |
| D6 | Total recovery and ridership share by transport mode are both important | Calculate `total_rides_k` and `mode_share` after filtering and standardising the mode data | This preserves the original chart's total-ridership context while revealing changes in Bus, MRT and LRT share |
| D7 | LRT is visually compressed in the original chart | Keep LRT as a normal mode and calculate indexed metrics rather than filtering it out | This makes LRT readable without pretending it has the same absolute scale as Bus or MRT |
| D8 | Monthly data may be useful but should not replace annual data | Treat the monthly LTA table as optional context or validation only | The proposed final chart is annual, so the official annual table remains the authoritative plotted source |
| D9 | Labels and rounded values can imply false precision | Calculate using the converted `daily_rides_k` values, then round only in display labels and tooltips | This keeps the analysis consistent while making the chart readable |

## 5.3 Target Output Schema

Three planned objects will support the visualization stage. `ridership_metrics` will store the cleaned and derived yearly metrics, `ridership_data` will support the optional metric-toggle chart, and `mode_labels` will hold endpoint labels and annotation positions so that the plotting code stays clean.

`ridership_metrics` - Primary Metrics Table

`ridership_metrics` stores the cleaned yearly ridership data and derived metrics before reshaping for the optional metric toggle.

| Column | R Type | Source | Unit / Range | Role in Chart |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `year` | integer | LTA yearly table | 2019-2025 | X-axis and time ordering |
| `mode` | factor | Cleaned from LTA `mode` column; optional derived total | Bus, MRT, LRT, optional Total | Colour grouping and line grouping |
| `ridership` | numeric | LTA yearly CSV | Full average daily rides | Raw value retained from source |
| `daily_rides_k` | numeric | Derived from `ridership / 1000` | Thousands of average daily rides | Absolute ridership value for tooltips and optional absolute view |
| `total_rides_k` | numeric | Derived from all modes in each year | Thousands of average daily rides | Total ridership context and mode-share denominator |
| `mode_share` | numeric | `daily_rides_k / total_rides_k` | 0-1 proportion | Tooltip value and optional share view |
| `baseline_2019_k` | numeric | 2019 value by mode | Thousands of average daily rides | Baseline used for recovery comparison |
| `recovery_index` | numeric | `daily_rides_k / baseline_2019_k * 100` | 2019 = 100 | Main y-axis in the default chart |
| `gap_from_2019_k` | numeric | `daily_rides_k - baseline_2019_k` | Thousands of average daily rides | Shows whether each mode is above or below pre-Covid level |
| `pct_change_from_2019` | numeric | `daily_rides_k / baseline_2019_k - 1` | Percentage change | Tooltip and summary analysis |
| `yoy_change_k` | numeric | Current year minus previous year by mode | Thousands of average daily rides | Recovery momentum tooltip and analysis |
| `yoy_pct_change` | numeric | Current year divided by previous year minus 1, by mode | Percentage change | Optional tooltip/context metric |
| `is_total` | logical | Derived | `TRUE` or `FALSE` | Allows the optional total line to be toggled without creating a separate chart |
| `label_rides_m` | character | Formatted from `daily_rides_k / 1000` | e.g. `"3.490M"` | Human-readable tooltip/display label |

The final `ridership_data` object will be created from `ridership_metrics` by reshaping selected metrics, such as `recovery_index`, `daily_rides_k` and `mode_share`, into `metric_type` and `metric_value` for the optional metric toggle.

`mode_labels` - Annotation Table

| Column | R Type | Source | Unit / Range | Role in Chart |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `year` | integer | Filtered from `ridership_metrics` | Usually 2025 | Places endpoint labels on the final year |
| `mode` | factor | `ridership_metrics` | Bus, MRT, LRT | Matches label to line colour/group |
| `recovery_index` | numeric | `ridership_metrics` | 2019 = 100 | Y-position for endpoint labels |
| `daily_rides_k` | numeric | `ridership_metrics` | Thousands of average daily rides | Used in label text |
| `gap_from_2019_k` | numeric | `ridership_metrics` | Thousands of average daily rides | Supports above/below-baseline endpoint text |
| `label_text` | character | Formatted from mode and recovery index | e.g. `"MRT: 103.1"` | Text displayed beside line endpoints |
| `label_position` | character | Derived/manual adjustment | left/right/above/below | Prevents label overlap in the final chart |

## 5.4 Illustrative Code Structure

The final code will follow this structure. The exact file path may change depending on the project folder setup, but the downstream transformations will remain the same.

```r
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(scales)

# Stage 1: ingest yearly ridership CSV.
raw_yearly_ridership <- read_csv(
  "data/raw/yearly_ave_daily_pt_ridership.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# Stage 2: filter years, standardise mode labels and create thousands unit.
clean_yearly_long <- raw_yearly_ridership |>
  filter(
    year >= 2019,
    year <= 2025,
    mode %in% c("Public Bus", "MRT", "LRT")
  ) |>
  mutate(
    mode = recode(mode, "Public Bus" = "Bus"),
    mode = factor(mode, levels = c("Bus", "MRT", "LRT")),
    ridership = as.numeric(ridership),
    daily_rides_k = ridership / 1000
  ) |>
  arrange(mode, year)

# Stage 3: validate basic structure and known values.
stopifnot(nrow(clean_yearly_long) == 21)
stopifnot(!anyNA(clean_yearly_long[c("year", "mode", "ridership", "daily_rides_k")]))
stopifnot(near(sum(filter(clean_yearly_long, year == 2025)$daily_rides_k), 7540))

# Stage 4: reusable function for recovery metrics.
add_recovery_metrics <- function(data) {
  data |>
    group_by(mode) |>
    arrange(year, .by_group = TRUE) |>
    mutate(
      baseline_2019_k = daily_rides_k[year == 2019],
      recovery_index = daily_rides_k / baseline_2019_k * 100,
      gap_from_2019_k = daily_rides_k - baseline_2019_k,
      pct_change_from_2019 = daily_rides_k / baseline_2019_k - 1,
      yoy_change_k = daily_rides_k - lag(daily_rides_k),
      yoy_pct_change = daily_rides_k / lag(daily_rides_k) - 1
    ) |>
    ungroup()
}

# Stage 5: calculate mode-level metrics.
mode_data <- clean_yearly_long |>
  group_by(year) |>
  mutate(total_rides_k = sum(daily_rides_k)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    mode_share = daily_rides_k / total_rides_k
  ) |>
  add_recovery_metrics()

# Stage 6: create optional total series for the same chart object.
total_data <- clean_yearly_long |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(
    ridership = sum(ridership),
    daily_rides_k = sum(daily_rides_k),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    mode = factor("Total", levels = c("Bus", "MRT", "LRT", "Total")),
    total_rides_k = daily_rides_k,
    mode_share = NA_real_
  ) |>
  add_recovery_metrics()

ridership_metrics <- bind_rows(mode_data, total_data) |>
  mutate(
    mode = factor(mode, levels = c("Bus", "MRT", "LRT", "Total")),
    is_total = mode == "Total",
    label_rides_m = number(daily_rides_k / 1000, accuracy = 0.001, suffix = "M")
  )

# Stage 7: prepare metric-toggle data for the same chart object.
ridership_data <- ridership_metrics |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(recovery_index, daily_rides_k, mode_share),
    names_to = "metric_type",
    values_to = "metric_value"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    metric_type = recode(
      metric_type,
      recovery_index = "Recovery Index",
      daily_rides_k = "Absolute Ridership",
      mode_share = "Ridership Share"
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(metric_value))

# Stage 8: endpoint labels for default recovery-index view.
mode_labels <- ridership_metrics |>
  filter(year == 2025, !is_total) |>
  mutate(
    label_text = paste0(mode, ": ", round(recovery_index, 1), " (2019=100)"),
    label_position = "right"
  )

# Stage 9: validation checks.
stopifnot(nrow(filter(ridership_metrics, !is_total)) == 21)
stopifnot(nrow(ridership_metrics) == 28)
stopifnot(all(ridership_metrics$recovery_index[ridership_metrics$year == 2019] == 100))
stopifnot(near(sum(filter(ridership_metrics, year == 2025, !is_total)$daily_rides_k), 7540))
```

## 5.5 Reproducibility Considerations

The workflow will be designed so that another team member can render the document and obtain the same chart outputs without editing paths or manually recalculating metrics.

| Area | Reproducibility Action |
|---|---|
| Source files | Keep the LTA DataMall yearly CSV as the main raw data file, and keep the LTA yearly PDF as a reference copy for checking values. If monthly context is used, keep the monthly PDF and note that the DataMall monthly CSV only covers up to 2024. Record retrieval dates and source links in the references. |
| Raw/processed separation | Store raw source files separately from cleaned and derived files. Processed outputs can be regenerated, but raw source files should not be edited directly. |
| File paths | Use project-relative paths such as `data/raw/` and `data/processed/` rather than absolute local paths. This allows the same Quarto file to render on different team members' machines. |
| Package use | Load all required packages in one setup block, including `readr`, `dplyr`, `tidyr`, `ggplot2`, `scales` and the selected interactive chart package. If time allows, package versions can be captured with `renv`. |
| Validation checks | Include row-count, missing-value, baseline, mode-share and known-value checks before plotting. The expected mode-only table size is 21 rows, or 28 rows if the optional total series is included. |
| Units and labels | Keep the raw CSV ridership values unchanged, create `daily_rides_k = ridership / 1000` for analysis, and create display labels separately. This prevents accidental mixing of analytical values and presentation formatting. |
| Traceability | Keep a data dictionary for `ridership_metrics`, `ridership_data` and `mode_labels`, including formulas for recovery index, mode share and gaps from 2019. |
| Determinism | The workflow uses fixed official values and deterministic transformations, so no random seed is required. Any manual label-position adjustments will be stored in `mode_labels`. |

# 6. Data Analysis Plan

## 6.1 What This Analysis Is For

The analysis plan supports the redesigned visualization by producing the exact metrics needed to explain the public transport recovery story. The goal is descriptive rather than predictive: we want to quantify what changed from 2019 to 2025, which transport mode recovered most strongly, and how ridership share by transport mode changed over time.

The analysis will answer three practical questions:

- **A1:** How far above or below the 2019 baseline is each transport mode by 2025?
- **A2:** How did each mode's share of total public transport ridership change between 2019 and 2025?
- **A3:** Which years show the strongest year-on-year recovery momentum after the 2020 drop?

## 6.2 Measures We Will Compute

The measures are deliberately simple so that they can be checked by hand and explained clearly in the visualization tooltips and annotations.

| Measure | Formula / Method | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Total ridership | `sum(daily_rides_k)` across Bus, MRT and LRT for each year | Overall average daily public transport ridership in thousands |
| Mode share | `daily_rides_k / total_rides_k` | The percentage contribution of each mode to total ridership in that year |
| 2019 baseline | For each mode, use its 2019 `daily_rides_k` value | Pre-Covid reference point for recovery comparison |
| Recovery index | `daily_rides_k / baseline_2019_k * 100` | Values above 100 exceed 2019 levels; values below 100 remain below 2019 |
| Gap from 2019 | `daily_rides_k - baseline_2019_k` | Absolute difference from pre-Covid ridership, in thousands of average daily rides |
| Percentage change from 2019 | `daily_rides_k / baseline_2019_k - 1` | Relative percentage change compared with the 2019 baseline |
| Year-on-year change | Current year's `daily_rides_k` minus previous year's value for the same mode | Shows the speed and direction of recovery between adjacent years |

## 6.3 Planned Analyses

The planned analyses map directly to the proposed interactive recovery-index chart. Each analysis produces either a calculated field for the chart, a small summary table, or an annotation used to guide the reader.

### A1 — Recovery Gap from 2019 by Mode

**Question:** By 2025, which transport modes have recovered to or exceeded their 2019 average daily ridership levels?

**Method:** For each mode, calculate `recovery_index`, `gap_from_2019_k` and `pct_change_from_2019` using 2019 as the fixed baseline.

**Expected output:** A 2025 summary table and endpoint labels for the recovery-index chart. Based on the current values, MRT should be above 2019, Bus should remain below 2019 and LRT should be almost unchanged from 2019.

**Insight supported:** This directly supports the main story that overall ridership nearly recovered, but the recovery was uneven across transport modes.

### A2 — Mode Share Shift Across Bus, MRT and LRT

**Question:** Did the balance between Bus, MRT and LRT change even though total public transport ridership nearly returned to its 2019 level?

**Method:** Calculate each mode's share of yearly total ridership, then compare 2019 and 2025 shares. The main comparison will be `mode_share_2025 - mode_share_2019`, reported in percentage points.

**Expected output:** A mode-share comparison table and tooltip values in the chart. The expected pattern is that Bus share decreases, MRT share increases and LRT remains a small share of the total.

**Insight supported:** This explains why total ridership alone is not enough. Similar total ridership can still hide changes in ridership share by transport mode.

### A3 — Year-on-Year Ridership Recovery Momentum

**Question:** After the sharp 2020 drop, which years show the strongest recovery momentum for each mode?

**Method:** Calculate `yoy_change_k` by mode and year. The largest positive changes will identify the years where ridership recovered most quickly, while negative values will flag setbacks such as the 2019 to 2020 fall.

**Expected output:** A compact table of the largest year-on-year increases by mode, plus tooltip fields showing year-on-year change for every point in the chart.

**Insight supported:** This adds time-path context to the final 2025 comparison. It helps readers see not only where ridership ended up, but how the recovery progressed across 2020 to 2025.

## 6.4 Summary: What We Produce (2019 to 2025)

| ID | Question Answered | Output | Supports Which Insight | Data Needed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | Which modes are above or below their 2019 ridership level? | Recovery-gap table, recovery-index values and 2025 endpoint labels | MRT exceeded 2019, Bus remained below 2019 and LRT was nearly unchanged | Yearly LTA ridership by mode, 2019-2025 |
| A2 | How did each mode's share of total ridership change? | 2019 vs 2025 mode-share comparison table and tooltip values | The total nearly recovered, but ridership share shifted toward MRT | Yearly LTA ridership by mode and yearly totals |
| A3 | Which years had the strongest recovery momentum after 2020? | Year-on-year change table and tooltip values | Recovery was gradual, with different speeds across Bus, MRT and LRT | Yearly LTA ridership by mode, ordered by year |

# 7. Planned Work Distribution

| Team Member | Tasks Done | Est. Hours |
|---|---|---|
| Thaw Zin Htun | To be confirmed by team |  |
| Siyona Shailesh Naik | To be confirmed by team |  |
| Wilson Teng Hao Feng | Refined proposal Sections 1 to 6; shaped the 2019 recovery story; revised chart critique, weakness IDs and redesign plan; checked LTA PDF/CSV sources; aligned Section 4 and 5 with the actual DataMall CSV structure; planned the data workflow, output schema, validation checks and analysis measures; supported Quarto formatting and render checks. | ~20 hrs |
| Troy | To be confirmed by team |  |
| Zayar | To be confirmed by team |  |
| All Members | Final content review and consistency checking | ~4 hrs each |

# 8. References

## 8.1 Article Source

- Chelvan, V. P. (2026, January 30). *More rides on MRT in 2025 as LRT use fell; bus ride numbers still below pre-Covid-19 levels*. The Straits Times. <https://www.straitstimes.com/singapore/transport/more-people-used-mrt-in-2025-as-lrt-ridership-falls-bus-numbers-still-below-pre-covid-levels>

This article is the source of the original news-media visualization and project story. It is used for critique and context, while the final plotted values will be sourced from LTA.

## 8.2 Data Sources

- Land Transport Authority. (n.d.). *Average public transport ridership by year (2015 to 2025)*. <https://www.lta.gov.sg/content/dam/ltagov/who_we_are/statistics_and_publications/statistics/pdf/PT_Ridership_Yearly_2015-2025.pdf>

- Land Transport Authority. (n.d.). *Average public transport ridership by month (2019 to 2025)*. <https://www.lta.gov.sg/content/dam/ltagov/who_we_are/statistics_and_publications/statistics/pdf/PT_Ridership_Monthly_2019-2025.pdf>

- Land Transport Authority. (n.d.). *LTA DataMall static datasets*. <https://datamall.lta.gov.sg/content/datamall/en/static-data.html>

The yearly DataMall ridership dataset is the main data source for the proposed chart. The yearly PDF is retained as the official reference table for value checking, and the monthly source may be used only for supporting context or validation.

## 8.3 Design Reference

- Wong, B. (2011). Points of view: Color blindness. *Nature Methods, 8*(6), 441–441. <https://doi.org/10.1038/nmeth.1618>

This reference supports the use of clearer colour contrast and a colourblind-conscious palette in the improved visualization.

# 9. Appendix: Data Dictionary

This appendix defines the planned visualization-ready data objects. It follows the Section 5 workflow and supports the Section 6 analysis plan. The `ridership_metrics` table stores the cleaned and derived yearly metrics. The `ridership_data` table is created from `ridership_metrics` if the optional metric toggle is implemented.

## A.1 `ridership_metrics` — Primary Metrics Table

`ridership_metrics` stores the cleaned yearly ridership data and derived metrics before reshaping for the optional metric toggle.

| Column                 | R Type    | Source / Derivation                                    | Unit / Range                     | Role in Chart                                      |
| ---------------------- | --------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| `year`                 | integer   | LTA yearly dataset                                     | 2019-2025                        | X-axis and time ordering                           |
| `mode`                 | factor    | Cleaned from LTA `mode` field                          | Bus, MRT, LRT, optional Total    | Line colour and grouping                           |
| `ridership`            | numeric   | LTA DataMall CSV                                       | Full average daily rides         | Raw value retained for traceability                |
| `daily_rides_k`        | numeric   | `ridership / 1000`                                     | Thousands of average daily rides | Absolute ridership values and tooltip label source |
| `total_rides_k`        | numeric   | Sum of Bus, MRT and LRT per year                       | Thousands of average daily rides | Denominator for mode share and total context       |
| `mode_share`           | numeric   | `daily_rides_k / total_rides_k`                        | 0-1 proportion                   | Ridership share metric and tooltip value           |
| `baseline_2019_k`      | numeric   | 2019 `daily_rides_k` by mode                           | Thousands of average daily rides | Baseline for recovery comparison                   |
| `recovery_index`       | numeric   | `daily_rides_k / baseline_2019_k * 100`                | 2019 = 100                       | Default y-axis metric                              |
| `gap_from_2019_k`      | numeric   | `daily_rides_k - baseline_2019_k`                      | Thousands of average daily rides | Shows above/below 2019 level                       |
| `pct_change_from_2019` | numeric   | `daily_rides_k / baseline_2019_k - 1`                  | Percentage change                | Tooltip and summary analysis                       |
| `yoy_change_k`         | numeric   | Current year minus previous year, by mode              | Thousands of average daily rides | Year-on-year recovery momentum                     |
| `yoy_pct_change`       | numeric   | Current year divided by previous year minus 1, by mode | Percentage change                | Optional tooltip/context metric                    |
| `is_total`             | logical   | Derived from `mode == "Total"`                         | `TRUE` or `FALSE`                | Supports optional total-line toggle                |
| `label_rides_m`        | character | Formatted from `daily_rides_k / 1000`                  | e.g. `3.490M`                    | Readable display label                             |

## A.2 `ridership_data` — Interactive Chart Table

`ridership_data` is created by reshaping selected metrics from `ridership_metrics` into a long format. This allows the same chart object to switch between recovery index, absolute ridership and ridership share if the metric toggle is implemented.

| Column                 | R Type    | Source / Derivation                   | Unit / Range                                        | Role in Chart                       |
| ---------------------- | --------- | ------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| `year`                 | integer   | From `ridership_metrics`              | 2019-2025                                           | X-axis and time ordering            |
| `mode`                 | factor    | From `ridership_metrics`              | Bus, MRT, LRT, optional Total                       | Line colour and grouping            |
| `is_total`             | logical   | From `ridership_metrics`              | `TRUE` or `FALSE`                                   | Supports optional total-line toggle |
| `metric_type`          | factor    | Derived by reshaping selected metrics | Recovery Index, Absolute Ridership, Ridership Share | Metric toggle option                |
| `metric_value`         | numeric   | Value associated with `metric_type`   | Depends on selected metric                          | Y-axis value for selected metric    |
| `label_rides_m`        | character | From `ridership_metrics`              | e.g. `3.490M`                                       | Exact ridership label in tooltip    |
| `gap_from_2019_k`      | numeric   | From `ridership_metrics`              | Thousands of average daily rides                    | Tooltip and annotation context      |
| `pct_change_from_2019` | numeric   | From `ridership_metrics`              | Percentage change                                   | Tooltip and analysis context        |
| `yoy_change_k`         | numeric   | From `ridership_metrics`              | Thousands of average daily rides                    | Tooltip showing year-on-year change |
| `yoy_pct_change`       | numeric   | From `ridership_metrics`              | Percentage change                                   | Optional tooltip/context metric     |

## A.3 `mode_labels` — Annotation Table

`mode_labels` stores endpoint labels and label-position decisions separately from the main data table. This keeps annotation logic out of the main plotting code.

| Column            | R Type    | Source / Derivation                    | Unit / Range                     | Role in Chart                               |
| ----------------- | --------- | -------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| `year`            | integer   | Filtered from `ridership_metrics`      | Usually 2025                     | Places labels at the end of each line       |
| `mode`            | factor    | Filtered from `ridership_metrics`      | Bus, MRT, LRT                    | Matches label to the correct line           |
| `recovery_index`  | numeric   | From `ridership_metrics`               | 2019 = 100                       | Y-position for default recovery-index view  |
| `daily_rides_k`   | numeric   | From `ridership_metrics`               | Thousands of average daily rides | Used in label text and tooltip              |
| `gap_from_2019_k` | numeric   | From `ridership_metrics`               | Thousands of average daily rides | Supports above/below-baseline label wording |
| `label_text`      | character | Formatted from mode and recovery index | e.g. `MRT: 103.1 (2019=100)`     | Text displayed beside line endpoints        |
| `label_position`  | character | Manual or derived adjustment           | right/left/above/below           | Reduces label overlap                       |

## A.4 Key Validation Checks

| Check                    | Expected Result                                                             | Why It Matters                                                           |
| ------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Mode-only row count      | 21 rows: 7 years x 3 modes                                                  | Confirms the 2019-2025 Bus/MRT/LRT subset is complete                    |
| Optional total row count | 28 rows if Total is included                                                | Confirms the optional total series has one row per year                  |
| Missing values           | No missing `year`, `mode`, `ridership`, `daily_rides_k` or `recovery_index` | Prevents broken lines, labels or tooltips                                |
| 2019 recovery index      | 100 for every mode                                                          | Confirms the baseline calculation is correct                             |
| 2025 total               | 7,540 thousand average daily rides                                          | Confirms the plotted 2025 values match the LTA and Straits Times figures |
| Mode share totals        | Bus + MRT + LRT shares sum to 1 for each year                               | Confirms mode-share calculations use the correct denominator             |
